title: Brazil LGPD AI Provisions and AI Regulation Bill
official_title: Lei Geral de Proteção de Dados Pessoais (LGPD) — AI-relevant provisions; and Brazil AI Bill (PL 2338/2023)
status: LGPD In Force; AI Bill advancing through Congress (2025)
overview: Brazil's AI legal landscape has two components: the existing LGPD (Brazilian General Data Protection Law), which is in force and directly governs AI systems processing personal data, including a specific Article 20 on automated decision-making; and PL 2338/2023 (the AI Bill), which passed the Senate in December 2024 and is advancing through the Chamber of Deputies. The AI Bill takes a risk-based approach modeled partly on the EU AI Act but with Brazil-specific adaptations including strong provisions on algorithmic discrimination and consumer rights. Brazil is the most significant AI regulatory market in Latin America.

lgpd_ai_obligations.enforcement_body: Autoridade Nacional de Proteção de Dados (ANPD)

key_provisions.title: Automated individual decision-making
key_provisions.description: Data subjects have the right to request review of automated decisions that affect their interests, including decisions based on profiling of natural persons.
obligations: Upon request, provide clear and adequate information about the criteria and procedures used for automated decisions
Implement a process for reviewing automated decisions affecting individuals
Disclose when decisions are made by automated means
Cannot make decisions based solely on automated processing that produce legal effects or significantly affect individuals without providing a review mechanism
key_provisions.comparison: Similar to GDPR Article 22 but applies more broadly — the right to explanation is not limited to legal or similarly significant effects

key_provisions.title: Data processing principles applicable to AI
obligations: Purpose: AI systems may only process data for specific, legitimate and explicit purposes informed to the data subject
Necessity: only minimum necessary personal data may be used in AI systems
Non-discrimination: AI processing cannot be carried out for illicit or abusive discriminatory purposes
Transparency: data subjects must have access to clear, accurate and accessible information about AI processing

ai_bill_pl_2338.overview: Brazil's AI Bill establishes a horizontal framework for AI systems, with risk classification, obligations for high-risk AI, prohibited practices, and consumer rights. It draws on the EU AI Act but includes Brazil-specific provisions on algorithmic discrimination, worker rights, and public sector AI use.

high_risk_categories: AI used in decisions on education access, credit, and social benefits
AI used in recruitment and employment
AI used in fundamental rights determinations
AI used by public authorities for law enforcement

proposed_obligations.title: Prior impact assessment
proposed_obligations.description: Operators of high-risk AI must conduct a prior impact assessment (similar to DPIA) before deployment.

proposed_obligations.title: Transparency and explanation
proposed_obligations.description: Operators must be transparent about AI use and provide explanations for significant automated decisions upon request.

proposed_obligations.title: Right to human review
proposed_obligations.description: Individuals have the right to request human review of significant AI-based decisions.

proposed_obligations.title: Anti-discrimination
proposed_obligations.description: AI systems must not discriminate on the basis of protected characteristics. This applies even to systems with discriminatory effect regardless of intent.

prohibited_practices: AI systems that manipulate individuals through subliminal or exploitative techniques
Social scoring by public authorities using AI
Real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces for law enforcement (with narrow exceptions)
AI systems that violate human dignity or fundamental rights

workers_rights.description: The AI Bill includes specific provisions on AI in employment that go beyond most other national frameworks
key_provisions: Employers must notify workers and unions when AI systems are used to monitor or manage workers
AI-based performance monitoring must comply with privacy and dignity protections
Workers affected by AI-driven layoffs have specific consultation rights

cross_references.relevance: Brazil AI Bill is substantially modeled on EU AI Act with adaptations for Brazil's legal context; companies with both EU and Brazil operations will find significant structural alignment.
LGPD is modeled on GDPR; Article 20 LGPD mirrors GDPR Article 22; GDPR-compliant AI governance substantially satisfies LGPD.
